item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love the interpretation of some of the other SCPs!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I quite like this, especially the nightmare imagery, and the plague apocalypse. One question: are the pile of sand and the woman with a black spot on her face in iteration 3 a reference to another article or articles? I'm drawing a blank, but it seemed like the specificity of the description was supposed to get the reader's attention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My greek mythology nerd side literally screamed when they saw the title, story doesn't dissapoint in the slightest either, it was really interesting and not tiring to read, which in my opinion, is a really important thing to achieve for a longer story/SCP. Also it's a really interesting take on Pandora's Box overall, I like how Mona lives a happy life at the end as well, really heart touching.

You've won a well deserved +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was planning on taking the next number up if my scp got passed, decided to check this one out and I'm very glad I did. I do have to ask, what exactly is the purpose for all out war to get the box? If they expect to find something in the box, what is it, and if not then is their goal simply to find out what it is that's in the box? I just don't quite understand why they want to box so badly, otherwise the other 4 stories are very well made. Love how it goes the 4 horsemen and then a "You Win" kind of story. Also love how you used the foundation as a foundation for them. Pun very much intended. Overall, beautiful writing, great read, and I'd love to see more articles you've written if you have any others. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not just you.

As time goes on, and literally thousands of articles become part of the mainsite, there's a growing need to innovate and expand on the core premise of SCPs. This can mean a loss of division between tales and articles, new classes, formats, and storytelling methods all together! Just like a Series 1 article wouldn't fly on the modern Wiki, with an increasing need for narrative and character development, you're going to start seeing more and more separation from the norm as time goes on.

You'll see this a lot more during contests, when your article is in direct competition with, like, 80-100 others. You need to work outside the box (pun intended). Don't worry, you're not alone if you're not super comfortable with this. A lot of people like the classic format, and classic focus, but a writing community has to evolve like anything else. Double don't worry though! Check my other works - they're almost entirely Safe and Euclid, and focused on the SCP itself!

Thanks for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it just me, or are SCP Tales getting directly included in SCP documents more and more, whereas they were on separate files before? Also, what is with the overuse of esoteric classes? Why does nearly everything have to cause the end of the world? What happened to Safe and Euclid objects? Why is the SCP object itself just an afterthought anymore?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good interpretation! I will say, I didn't intend this to all be a "dream" of the D-Class. More that opening the box fundamentally changed how the D-Class perceived reality. Thanks so much for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hah! I really like that! Not my original intention, but it's a very solid interpretation :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely beautifully written. The title fits perfectly.

SPOILERS
My Interpretation

I thought right away about the last thing being "hope", something we all deserve. Once it was released, madness would overtake humanity, as everyone lost their will to continue. The result would be the Foundation losing its ability to care for its facilities, leading to the release of almost all SCPs, benign and dangerous. Or could it be that the tales in this SCP are simply the dreams of the D-class? These tormenting nightmares make her think "wow, that sucks, so carpe diem", which is why she had such a good life?


All in all, one of my favorite articles to date.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually developed an interpretation of my own shortly after writing my comment here.

📥Spoilers, probably.📥
📤Spoilers, probably.📤

Essentially, it goes like: Opening the box unleashes disaster, as anyone would expect of Pandora's Box. However, this disaster is unleashed on the "next universe in line", rather than the universe you're in. Iteration 1 is getting apocalypse'd because someone opened the box in Iteration 0. Iteration 2 is getting apocalypse'd because someone opened the box in Iteration 1. Iteration 0, presumably, means that Iteration Negative 1 (which we don't see) had someone open the box.

But nobody opened the box in Iteration 3, because everyone died first. Because of that, Iteration 4 isn't stricken by any manner of cataclysm, and the note inside the box reflects that. (However, if there was an Iteration 5, it would be totally screwed.)

And I don't think this interpretation is incompatible with your intention. In this case, the terrible events themselves are literally inside the box. If the box is opened, the events are free to occur… in the next universe, at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The apocalypse of Hunger. Everyone on earth had their metabolism supercharged, we devoured the world, and now the only survivors are the "Starving Ones", people on the verge of starvation but cannot die, and those who are willing to do whatever it takes for a meal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh that makes sense! I overall really liked and that explains a lot. one question though. what was the first apocalypse? it seemed to have an interesting story but I couldn't quite figure it out

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks so much! I'm proud of that part too. As for the premise, here you go!

SPOILERS
SPOILED!

The Foundation, literally the writing site we're talking on, operates on a logic of "There is no canon". This is a meta-narrative on that. It's an object that contains "What If" scenarios. Literally contains, like a containment device! It holds potential canon inside! And when it's opened..

These are just five what-ifs for the end of the world, and one not. The box isn't Pandora, the story is Pandora.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey Christopoulos!

I tend to agree with you, but not in this circumstance. These stories were snap-shots of four apocalypses in motion, and the SCP community has already explored some of these core concepts famously. If ever there was a time to pay homage to the plague doctor, or the dead world, it was a 5k contest that dealt with Four Horsemen inspired scenarios. I'm glad the article itself landed for you, despite this gripe. Thanks for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That description of the dream is magnificent. I'm not sure I even quite understand the premise of this one, beyond the whole "Pandora's box" thing, but even just the dream alone is more than enough for me. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very cool article. Nice work.

The crosslinks to other SCPs actually took away from the article for me. I might be alone in that opinion, but I've always felt that an article that works without leaning on other articles at all is ideal. In this specific article's case, being a meta riff on canon, it's not nearly as bad a thing, but I honestly groan every time I hover over a link to 049 or 2000, among others.

Either way, my single gripe with the article isn't enough to make me not enjoy it. Easy +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got you fam!

SPOILERS
SPOILED!

The Foundation, literally the writing site we're talking on, operates on a logic of "There is no canon". This is a meta-narrative on that. It's an object that contains "What If" scenarios. Literally contains, like a containment device! It holds potential canon inside! And when it's opened..

These are just five what-ifs for the end of the world, and one not. The box isn't Pandora, the story is Pandora.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can anyone give me a quick rundown of what this means? The only thing that comes to mind is that the box unleashes some sorta apocalypse most of the time except the d-class "won" by having it unleash nothing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey yazshu! I agree on all points. "Mystery" here isn't the central focus of the piece, just a core element. Also, I also try to avoid cross-linking popular articles, but I figured if ever there was a time to pay homage to some of my favorite scips, a 5K would be it. Thanks so much for the read!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really love this one, it's probably my favorite of the contest so far. Although I feel that it the way its written may stray a little bit too out of the core theme of mystery, I think that it actually works more in advantage to the piece (in a similar way to Taboo). Some people might be a little bit offset by the reliance on crosslinking pretty popular articles for the scenarios, but I think it works well enough because the post-apocalypse writing stands out on its own. Which with how many post-apocalypse stories there are on the site, the fact that you managed to make it stand out so well with the overarching themes REALLY makes it good. Really love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks so much!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just a wonderful story. Good references, and a refreshing shift away from the usual SCP formula. You've got my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks so much for the read ShamrockJolnes! You're completely right. Many readers will be put off by the tale-like structure of this piece, but I figured if there was ever a time for non-conventional formatting, it'd be a 5k. I also appreciate everything you said, but honestly, this was a really rushed entry. A lot of the prose, dialogue, and story elements could have used many, many more hours of fine tuning and editing for a more compelling, flowing narrative. I simply didn't have enough time this year, and a K contest deserves more than that.

I'll be back on my monthly posting schedule soon, and I hope my next piece has a smoother landing than this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to say, I'm just incredibly impressed by this entry. The setup for all the different iterations is clever (and captures the whole "there-is-no-canon" spirit in the best way), and the execution of the various end-of-the-world tales is just top-notch throughout. The prose is compelling, and each entry does a great job of capturing that feeling of end-of-the-world desperation in a short space (with some especially effective uses of understatement, like the 049 crosslink and the whole Death/2935 section).

I'm sure there'll be some of the traditional complaints about how "this reads more like a tale than a SCP file," but the way this article plays around with the standard format feels anything but arbitrary. Rather, it's a means to the end of telling an effective story, one that wouldn't have quite so memorable of an impact in a more standard format. The ending flash-forward caught me by surprise, but it definitely highlights the themes you referenced in your author post. All in all, an easy +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the crit, Soulless. I'm sorry this didn't land for you. I'll review and revise, and hopefully you'll find my next piece more palettable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bunch of good stories that make for a quietly beautiful piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

The act was maddening; always chewing, never swallowing, but it kept him from a deeper madness.

This sentence is awkward I think.

Joshua packed up his camp and made his way down the silent highway; a nettle-grove of empty cars and twisted rebar.

The semicolon doesn't work here; semicolons are used to join complete clauses.

He was using the highway for it's intended purpose; a quick route from here to there.

Same for this.

He'd been staring at the window, and had forgotten his surroundings.

I would recommend removing explaining the exactness of Joshua's thoughts and internal state. You speak more powerfully with action than in explanation.

They were coming for him now.

This similar. You don't need to explain to me the meaning of events when they are easily gleaned by their happenings.

Birds would have taken wing, flying up from the nearby trees… but of course, neither had existed in a long, long time.

I think this would've been better stated to simply state the absence of response.

They never had much neck on them, just a thin chicken bone propping up the head.

Pick one of the two here.

Useless.

At this point I'd like for you to take an evaluation of what tools you are using to describe. What are your senses you're using?

He chewed so hard his gums began to bleed.

Again, you can do a lot more here. There's taste of blood. There's smell of fat. There's a dirty rag being chewed on for hours and hours. It coats the throat. It chafes the cheeks. Push.

dark red liquid

You have an opportunity here to write a stronger adjective in the place of two weak ones.

, just performing your own blood test

Remove 'just'

She didn’t have any choice. There was no one else left.

Unecessary again. At the point where someone is pulling marrow out of themselves it's clear they're out of options.

Again, in this scene, not enough senses for a space so greusome. Imagine the smell of the rot. Imagine how the disease lingers in the air like a mucousy film.

He wore an unmistakable expression. Otto chose his next words much more carefully.

I don't think this is necessary either.

She became a certified accountant, and her co-workers quickly realized that, for whatever reason, Mona knew how to handle stress like a hardened soldier. She was running the company in less than a decade.

I would suggest a particualrly stressful subfield of accounting, because a layman would not understand why stress management is useful here.

Another issue, architecturally, is that there isn't really change or progress in the first scene also. In general, I think each individual scene wasn't strong enough to carry the overall piece for me. I referenced in the collapsible a variety of strategies that could be used to make the prose tighter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, you want to hear another story, eh? One where the very fate of Pandora hangs in the balance?

No? Well too bad I'm telling you anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I encourage it, it's a great article!

The original had it as is, without the cave reference, implying when she opened the chest everyone and everything simply died. When I realized that we already had a skip that explored this scenario, I made the reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1.
I guess that the death one is a strong reference to the cave skip, I'll read it next

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was more worried about a James Cameron vibe!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why, but I got Borderlands vibes upon seeing the title.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you are writing any book about the end of the world, what you are really writing about is what's worth saving about it.

- Justin Cronin

Image Credits
Image Credits

Image source: All three images of the casket are used with permission, and provided by Rob Michiels Auctions.

https://www.rm-auctions.com/en/winter-auction-2018/13395-a-rectangular-siculo-arabic-ivory-casket-sicily-13-14th-c

3.0 Unported CC BY 3.0 use of these images for use in this article, and for hosting on the SCP Foundation Website were given by Jeanne Raes via email. An email log has been provided to ProcyonLotor, and been confirmed.

https://www.pxfuel.com/en/free-photo-ervre Creative Commons Zero - CC0
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Claw_Marks.png
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Naturalis_Biodiversity_Center_-_RMNH.ART.604_-_Centipede_-_Kawahara_Keiga_-_1823_-_1829_-_Siebold_Collection_-_pencil_drawing_-_water_colour.jpeg
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Naturalis_Biodiversity_Center_-_RMNH.ART.594_-_Tabanus_sp._-_Kawahara_Keiga_-_1823_-_1829_-_Siebold_Collection_-_pencil_drawing_-_water_colour.jpeg
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Naturalis_Biodiversity_Center_-_RMNH.ART.591_-_Homoeogryllus_japonicus_-_Kawahara_Keiga_-_1823_-_1829_-_Siebold_Collection_-_pencil_drawing_-_water_colour.jpeg All under 3.0

This is my take on the apocalypse, combining the Four Horsemen mythos, the Pandora's Box mythos, and a bit of Foundation lore sprinkled in. The obvious mystery is what's in the box? To those of you out there who just read that in the "Seven" Brat Pitt movie voice, bless you.

The second layer of mystery is the question posed throughout the article. What is most important when the world is ending? Like so many other 5000 entries, I leave the answer up to you.

This was a very late entry; the last two months have been incredibly busy for me, leaving very little time for writing. As such, I didn't have time to run this past the many excellent authors on this site I usually work with. To you, I'm sorry, and I can't wait to collaborate and critique again in the future.

To everyone else, I hope you enjoyed the read.

► More T Rutherford Articles and Tales ◄
